variables.USER_FOCUS: High-intensity adversarial review for Mantle V2, an OP Stack-based rollup monorepo with Go services, Solidity contracts, fault proofs, alternative data availability, and MNT-native protocol assumptions.
REPOSITORY_PROFILE: Go and Solidity monorepo for Mantle V2, an OP Stack-based Mantle rollup implementation.
Go module: github.com/ethereum-optimism/optimism; Go toolchain is tracked in go.mod.

Major components include contracts-bedrock, op-node, op-batcher, op-proposer, op-challenger, op-program, op-service, op-chain-ops, op-deployer, op-supervisor, op-conductor, gas-oracle, op-alt-da, cannon, devnet and acceptance-test tooling.

Mantle-specific protocol context includes the upgrade sequence BaseFee -> Everest -> Euboea -> Skadi -> Limb -> Arsia, OP Stack fork alignment, a Mantle L1 data fee model, EigenDA/alternative DA support, and MNT as the native token.
Build and checks may involve Go tests, Solidity/Foundry contract tests, e2e tests, acceptance tests, devnet/Kurtosis workflows, linters, and Makefile targets.

Public contracts: rollup derivation rules, chain configuration, genesis/deploy config, batch submission behavior, output root/proposal behavior, dispute game/fault proof interfaces, preimage oracle behavior, contract ABIs/storage layouts, service CLI flags, metrics, and operational runbooks.

Consensus and bridge risk areas: deposits, withdrawals, cross-domain messaging, output roots, L1/L2 reorg handling, finality, fault proofs, dispute games, preimage data, and upgrade activation boundaries.

Data availability and fee risk areas: EigenDA/alt-DA commitments, blob/calldata selection, gas oracle updates, L1 data fee calculation, MNT-vs-ETH assumptions, and fee accounting under partial failures.

Operational risk areas: batcher/proposer/challenger/conductor liveness, retries, idempotency, key/secret handling, RPC dependency degradation, metrics/logging visibility, and safe recovery after interrupted operations.
Compatibility risk areas: upstream OP Stack drift, chain config mismatches, contract upgrade safety, generated bindings, deployment scripts, and devnet assumptions leaking into production paths.
Review should treat protocol, contract, chain-config, and service-liveness changes as high blast-radius.
